aligned_pages.ppt_text:
Training Error and Generalization Error
• Training error (bias): model error on the training data
• Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
• Example: practice a future exam with past exams
• Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn’t guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
• Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers

aligned_segments.text: And this introduce our classic discussion of training error and generalization error.
Training error means, the error on the training data set and generalization error refers to your error on a new data.
And we always say that the performance on the training data does not represents the performance on a new data set.

This is very similar to we make practices for a future exam with past exams and for those who do well on past exam, that means you get a very small training error that does not guarantee a good score on the future exam where the future exam actually represent the generalization error, right?

May get zero error on pass exam by root learning.
That means he remember all the answers mechanically, but that student may have a word score compared to another student who understand the reasons for a given number answers.

• The ability to fit variety offunctions
• Low-capacity models struggles to fit trainingset
• High-capacity models can memorize the training set

• It’s hard to compare complexity between different algorithms

• Given an algorithm family, two main factors matter:
• The number of parameters
• The values taken by each parameter

aligned_segments.text: The next thing is how to estimate the complexity of the model.
In general, it is difficult to compare complexity between different models.

The first weight is you can compare the number of parameters.
So how many weight parameters do you have in this model?
For example, if you compare the single linear regression model with the multilayer perception, your multilayer perception definitely have more parameters.
So we say that multilayer perception is a more complex model.

• It’s hard to compare complexity between different algorithms

• Given an algorithm family, two main factors matter:
• The number of parameters
• The values taken by each parameter

aligned_segments.text: The next thing is how to estimate the complexity of the model.
In general, it is difficult to compare complexity between different models.
For example, it's hard to tell whether a tree model is more complex or not compared to a new network.
So on the other hand, given, algorithm, family new network, it is possible to compare the model complexity in two ways.
The first weight is you can compare the number of parameters.
So how many weight parameters do you have in this model?
For example, if you compare the single linear regression model with the multilayer perception, your multilayer perception definitely have more parameters.
So we say that multilayer perception is a more complex model.
Okay, and also, the other consideration is the value taken by each parameter.
So maybe my model could have a lot of parameters.
However, maybe many of the parameters share the same value.
For example, in some models, there are a lot of zeros, and you can imagine if you have a lot of zeros, then your model is actually simpler than a model that has all different numbers.
And in the other case, next class, we'll talk about something called convolutional new network in that model.
Actually, there are a lot of weight parameter that share the same value.
Because they share the same value.
You actually don't need to use too many numbers to describe the model.
So those type of model are actually, , simpler compared to a fully, connected multi layer perception.
This is about how we compare a model complexity.

For example, the number of data points definitely matters for the complexity.
